questions.question: You're proving by induction that `P(n)` holds for all n ≥ 1. You've verified the base P(1) and shown that P(k) implies P(k+1). What did you just prove?
questions.explanation: Induction gives you exactly the claim from the base onward. If your base is P(1), you've covered n ≥ 1. P(0) is NOT proved (you didn't verify it). Always match the base case to the claim's starting point.

questions.question: Why is proof by contrapositive equivalent to direct proof of the same claim?
options: It isn't — they prove different things
Because `P → Q ≡ ¬Q → ¬P` is a tautology of propositional logic
questions.explanation: Logical equivalence: P → Q and ¬Q → ¬P have identical truth tables. Proving one proves the other. Use whichever is easier — often the contrapositive is, because it lets you start from the 'negative' side.

questions.question: Euclid's proof that there are infinitely many primes is which kind of proof?
options: By contradiction (assume only finitely many, derive a contradiction)
questions.explanation: Classic contradiction. Assume the negation ('only finitely many primes p₁..pₙ exist'). Construct N = ∏pᵢ + 1. N can't be divisible by any of the pᵢ (remainder 1), so either it's a new prime or has a prime factor not in the list — contradiction in either case.

questions.question: When is strong induction *strictly more useful* than ordinary induction?
options: When proving P(k+1) requires assuming P(m) for some m < k, not just P(k); the prime-factorization proof is a typical example
Only when n ranges over rationals
questions.explanation: Ordinary induction lets you assume only P(k) when proving P(k+1). Strong induction lets you assume P(b) through P(k). They're mutually derivable (you can rewrite either as the other), but for claims like 'every n ≥ 2 has a prime factorization' — where k splits as a · b with 1 < a, b < k — strong induction is the natural fit because you invoke the IH on a and b, neither of which is k-1.

questions.question: Structural induction is induction on what?
options: The recursive structure of a datatype: prove the property for base constructors (leaf/empty), then prove it for composite constructors assuming it holds for sub-structures
The number of variables in a formula
The first letter of a function name
questions.explanation: For a datatype defined recursively (lists, trees, expressions, ASTs), the induction principle follows the constructors. Base case = the property holds at base constructors. Step = the property holds at a composite constructor given that it holds for sub-pieces. This is THE main proof technique in PL theory (type soundness, evaluator correctness).

questions.question: You see a proof of `P(n)` by induction that has no base case. What's most likely wrong?
options: The 'proof' is incomplete — without a base, the induction doesn't even fire; you've proved 'IF P(0) then ∀n. P(n)' but not P(0)
questions.explanation: Induction is a two-part argument: P(0) AND (∀k. P(k) → P(k+1)). Without P(0), you've proved nothing — the dominos can't fall if the first isn't tipped. Always state and verify the base. This is the single most common induction bug in homework.
